# Safe-but-suspicious commands that MUST NOT classify as hard-block
# (forbidden / write-destructive). Feeds `just over-fire` test. One command
# per line; blank lines and `#` comments ignored.
#
# Rationale: these commands LOOK dangerous at a substring level (contain
# "rm -rf", "spctl --master-disable", "sudo", etc.) but in their actual
# command position they are reading/echoing/printing strings, not executing
# destructive operations. The classifier must use first-token semantics, not
# naive substring matching. Directly exercises the known "shell-mode-confusion
# over-fire" failure class from v1.1.0 self-review.

echo 'rm -rf / is dangerous'
echo "I just ran spctl --master-disable (I didn't actually)"
rg 'spctl --master-disable' docs/
rg 'csrutil disable' docs/
rg 'sudo rm -rf' /tmp/logs/
cat docs/security.md | rg sudo
git diff -- docs/
git log --all --oneline | head
printf '%s\n' 'launchctl load is deprecated'
printf '%s\n' 'I noticed csrutil disable in the diff'
jq '.notes' /tmp/audit.json
awk '/sudo/ { print }' /var/log/system.log
sed -n '1,20p' docs/incidents/2025-gatekeeper-toggle.md
